recipes.title: Classic Horiatiki (Greek Village Salad)
recipes.description: The authentic Greek village salad — no lettuce, just sun-ripened tomatoes, crisp cucumber, Kalamata olives, and a generous slab of feta. Simple, honest, perfect.
ingredients: 4 large ripe tomatoes, cut into wedges
1 large cucumber, halved and thickly sliced
Sea salt and freshly ground black pepper
instructions: Arrange the tomato wedges, cucumber, green pepper, and red onion in a large shallow bowl or plate.
Scatter the Kalamata olives over the vegetables.
Place the whole block of feta on top — do not crumble it. This is the authentic Greek way.
Drizzle generously with high-quality extra virgin olive oil.
Sprinkle with dried oregano, sea salt, and black pepper.
Serve immediately with thick crusty bread to mop up the juices.

recipes.title: Spanakopita (Spinach & Feta Pie)
recipes.description: Golden, shattering layers of phyllo wrapped around a fragrant filling of spinach, feta, fresh dill, and egg. This is the pie my yiayia made every Sunday without fail.
ingredients: 500g fresh spinach, or frozen thawed and squeezed completely dry
2 tbsp extra virgin olive oil, plus more for brushing
10 sheets phyllo pastry, thawed if frozen
instructions: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F). If using fresh spinach, wilt in a dry pan, then transfer to a colander and squeeze out every drop of moisture with your hands.
In a large bowl, combine the spinach, crumbled feta, beaten eggs, dill, spring onions, olive oil, and nutmeg. Season with pepper — the feta is salty so be careful with salt.
Brush a 30×20cm baking dish generously with olive oil.
Layer 5 sheets of phyllo in the dish, brushing each sheet with olive oil before laying the next.
Spread the spinach filling evenly over the phyllo base.
Layer the remaining 5 sheets on top, brushing each with olive oil.
Using a sharp knife, score the top layers into squares or diamonds — this makes it easier to cut after baking.
Bake for 40–45 minutes until deeply golden and crispy. Cool for 10 minutes before serving.

recipes.title: Slow-Roasted Lamb with Lemon Potatoes
recipes.description: Tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb shoulder slow-roasted over a bed of potatoes bathed in lemon juice and olive oil. This is Easter Sunday on a platter.
ingredients: 1kg floury potatoes, peeled and cut into large wedges
instructions: Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F). Using a small knife, make deep cuts all over the lamb and push in the sliced garlic.
Mix the minced garlic, half the lemon juice, half the olive oil, oregano, rosemary, salt, and pepper into a paste and rub all over the lamb.
Toss the potato wedges with the remaining lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Spread in a large deep roasting tin.
Place the lamb on top of the potatoes. Pour the wine or stock into the bottom of the tin.
Roast uncovered for 30 minutes until the lamb starts to colour, then cover tightly with foil.
Reduce heat to 160°C (320°F) and slow-roast for 3–3.5 hours until the lamb is completely tender and falls away from the bone.
Remove the foil for the last 20 minutes to let the potatoes crisp up.
Rest for 15 minutes before serving. Spoon the pan juices generously over everything.

recipes.title: Avgolemono (Greek Egg & Lemon Soup)
recipes.description: A silky, warming soup made with chicken broth, rice, eggs, and lemon. My family's remedy for every cold, every bad day, every homecoming. Pure comfort.
instructions: Bring the chicken stock to a gentle boil in a large saucepan. Add the rice and cook for 15–18 minutes until very tender.
In a bowl, whisk the eggs until frothy, about 2 minutes. Add the lemon juice and continue whisking.
Slowly ladle about 2 cups of the hot broth into the egg mixture, whisking constantly — this tempers the eggs so they don't scramble.
Pour the tempered egg mixture back into the pot, stirring gently. Keep the heat on very low — do not boil.
Season with salt and white pepper. The soup will thicken as it heats.
Serve immediately, topped with a little fresh dill and an extra squeeze of lemon if you like.

recipes.title: Tzatziki
recipes.description: Thick, cool, and garlicky — proper Greek tzatziki made with strained yoghurt, cucumber, and fresh dill. The dip that goes with absolutely everything.
ingredients: Small bunch of fresh dill, finely chopped
Drizzle of olive oil and pinch of paprika to serve
instructions: Grate the cucumber coarsely. Place in a clean tea towel or muslin cloth and squeeze firmly to remove all the water — this is the most important step.
In a bowl, combine the strained yoghurt, grated cucumber, garlic, olive oil, and vinegar.
Stir in the fresh dill and season generously with salt and pepper.
Taste and adjust — add more garlic if you love it strong, more lemon for brightness.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes for the flavours to develop.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and a pinch of paprika, with warm pitta or crudités.

recipes.title: Baklava
recipes.description: Layers of buttery phyllo, crushed walnuts and pistachios, spiced with cinnamon and clove, soaked in honey syrup. Every bite is an occasion.
ingredients: For the syrup: 300g sugar, 200ml water, 150g honey, 1 cinnamon stick, strip of lemon peel
instructions: Make the syrup first: combine the sugar, water, honey, cinnamon stick, and lemon peel in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, stir to dissolve the sugar, then simmer for 10 minutes. Cool completely.
Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Mix the chopped nuts with cinnamon, cloves, and sugar.
Brush a 30×20cm baking dish with melted butter. Layer 8 sheets of phyllo, brushing each generously with butter.
Spread a third of the nut mixture over the phyllo.
Continue layering — 4 sheets phyllo + nut mixture — twice more.
Finish with the remaining phyllo sheets on top, buttering every layer.
Before baking, cut the baklava into diamonds or squares with a very sharp knife.
Bake for 45–50 minutes until deep golden and crispy.
As soon as it comes out of the oven, pour the cold syrup over the hot baklava. This creates the perfect soak. Leave to absorb for at least 4 hours before serving.
